title: Quiz: Simulation-Based Testing
description: Test your understanding of quantum simulation testing strategies.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of statevector simulation for testing?

options.text: It runs faster than shot-based simulation
It provides exact state amplitudes without sampling noise

questions.explanation: Statevector simulation computes exact amplitudes, allowing precise verification without the statistical uncertainty of shot-based measurements.

questions.question: What is the memory requirement for statevector simulation of n qubits?
questions.explanation: Statevector simulation requires storing 2^n complex amplitudes, making memory grow exponentially with qubit count.

questions.question: What does a depolarizing noise channel do to a quantum state?

options.text: Rotates the state around the Z axis
Mixes the state with the maximally mixed state
Projects the state onto computational basis

questions.explanation: Depolarizing channel replaces the state with the maximally mixed state (identity/dimension) with some probability, modeling random errors.

questions.question: How does statistical uncertainty in measurement scale with shot count?

options.text: Proportional to square root of shots (1/sqrt(n))

questions.explanation: Statistical standard error scales as 1/sqrt(n), so quadrupling shots only halves the uncertainty.

questions.question: What is readout error in quantum measurement?

options.text: Incorrect classification of qubit state during measurement

questions.explanation: Readout error occurs when measuring a qubit in state |0> returns 1 or vice versa, due to measurement apparatus imperfections.

questions.question: Density matrix simulation can represent mixed quantum states that statevector simulation cannot.
questions.explanation: Density matrices can represent mixed states (statistical mixtures of pure states), while statevectors only represent pure states.

questions.question: When cross-validating simulators, what does total variation distance measure?
questions.explanation: Total variation distance is half the sum of absolute differences between probabilities, measuring how different two distributions are.
